HomeReady. Home-Readiness Report

Verify the home before discharge — agent-conducted, STEADI-aligned · run 113216-766172
PatientMonica Hilpert, 76F
ContextSNF admission — rehabilitation and pain management → home Friday
Findings27 hazards (10 critical)
Plan status1 obligation(s) blocked
Frames analyzed41

1 · Care-plan obligations — derived from the encounter documentation

HomeReady read the SNF note + after-visit summary (Abridge-style artifacts) and derived what must be true at home for this plan to work, then scored each against walkthrough evidence.

BLOCKED Ensure the 28-inch front-wheeled walker is present, correctly fitted, and used for all standing and ambulation at home.
note: “uses a 28in front-wheeled walker”
→ Measurement shows a doorway is only 27.6in wide while Monica's walker is 28in wide, so it will NOT fit through this door.
AT RISK Verify Monica can perform standing transfers and short-distance ambulation safely without another person present before she is left alone at home.
note: “Stands only with support and a fixed target; transfers slowly with assistance; ambulation currently limited to short supervised distances.”
→ The entry/hallway has a narrow doorway passage with a low metal rack 'appearing to leave limited clearance for a 28-inch-wide walker,' along with uneven flooring transitions that would impede safe unassisted ambulation.
UNVERIFIED Establish a home equivalent of the 'call-before-standing' safeguard (e.g., a call/alert device, phone within reach, or scheduled check-ins) since she will not have a facility call bell after discharge.
note: “Strict call-before-standing instruction reinforced; call bell education provided and accepted”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
AT RISK Remove or mitigate home fall hazards (loose rugs, poor lighting, clutter, unstable furniture) given her osteoporosis and fall risk.
note: “Osteoporosis and fall prevention”
→ Multiple critical hazards were found in the walking path, including an abrupt carpet-to-subfloor transition with a raised lip, a low black metal rack at ankle height, an A-frame sign lying across the path, an exposed electrical cord, and a stairway with no handrail and no step contrast marking.
UNVERIFIED Confirm bathing and dressing areas have safety equipment (grab bars, shower chair, raised toilet seat, etc.) consistent with OT-taught techniques.
note: “Occupational therapy sessions for dressing, bathing safety, and daily tasks”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
UNVERIFIED Confirm that social work has actually arranged home supports (e.g., check-ins, aide, community services) before she is left alone Friday.
note: “Social work to arrange home supports before discharge and to connect her with community or senior-center programs”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
UNVERIFIED Verify the neighbor who provides her only regular help is aware of her discharge and available to check on her, especially given she will be alone.
note: “a neighbor provides her only regular help”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
UNVERIFIED Ensure someone monitors Monica for sedation, confusion, or unsteadiness after meperidine doses since she will be alone without nursing observation.
note: “to be used sparingly with close nursing observation for sedation, confusion, or unsteadiness given her age”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
UNVERIFIED Confirm Monica has a plan to take pain medication ahead of planned activity/therapy exercises at home, as was done in the facility.
note: “Time analgesia ahead of therapy sessions to enable participation”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
UNVERIFIED Ensure her home food supply and meal preparation accommodate her one-sided chewing and soft-texture, protein/calcium-forward diet.
note: “Dietitian consultation for texture-modified, protein- and calcium-forward meals balanced for glucose and lipids”
→ not assessed during walkthrough
AT RISK Confirm a formal pre-discharge assessment of her mobility, medication management, meal management, and home setup was completed and passed before releasing her home alone.
note: “Formal pre-discharge assessment of mobility, medication and meal management, and home setup prior to release; discharge home once safe”
→ The walkthrough (a pre-discharge home assessment) documented numerous critical hazards—including an impassable doorway for the walker, an unguarded stairway, and floor debris/cords—indicating the home has not yet passed a safety review.
UNVERIFIED Confirm Monica can independently and correctly manage her naproxen and meperidine dosing schedule without nursing supervision.
note: “medication and meal management”
→ not assessed during walkthrough

2 · Escalations — routed to owners with evidence and deadlines

CLINICAL EXCEPTION → OT + discharging MD due: before Friday (discharge)

Expected Prescribed 28in front-wheeled walker usable throughout the home

Observed LiDAR: entry door width measured 27.6in — walker does not fit

Why Monica cannot reach this room with her prescribed mobility device; with osteoporosis, unsupported ambulation risks a fragility fracture. The discharge plan as written will not work.

Attempted Measurement verified during walkthrough; caregiver shown the reading on-screen

Next Reassess equipment (narrow-frame walker / hemi-walker), home modification, or discharge destination before Friday

OPERATIONAL EXCEPTION → Discharge coordinator + family due: before Friday (discharge)

Expected Clear, supported walking path through entry, stairs

Observed 10 critical hazards documented with photos (Abrupt flooring transition from carpet to bare, worn subfloo; Metal transition strip/threshold at the carpet-to-bare-subfl; Low black metal frame/rack object sitting directly on the fl…)

Why Each is an immediate fall mechanism for a deconditioned walker user with osteoporosis.

Attempted Caregiver was walked through each hazard during the session and given fix-in-place recommendations

Next Confirm hazards cleared (photo re-check) before Friday

ROUTINE EXCEPTION → Home-health PT/OT due: first home visit

Expected Home exercise + transfer plan adapted to the actual home

Observed Full graded walkthrough report with photos and measurements attached

Why Layout-specific transfer training beats generic instructions.

Attempted —

Next Use report findings to target the first PT/OT home session

3 · Graded findings

CRITICAL entry Floor surfaces/walking path condition

Abrupt flooring transition from carpet to bare, worn subfloor/concrete with visible debris and uneven patching in the main walking path

An uneven, unfinished floor transition can catch a front-wheeled walker's wheels and cause a fall, which for her osteoporotic bones risks a hip or vertebral fracture.

→ Level and finish the flooring transition (secure transition strip flush with both surfaces) before she uses this path; remove loose debris.

CRITICAL entry Floor surfaces/transitions - trip hazards

Metal transition strip/threshold at the carpet-to-bare-subfloor junction creates a raised lip between two different flooring surfaces in the main walking path.

A raised threshold strip is exactly the type of surface irregularity that can catch a front-wheeled walker's caster and cause a fall, which for her osteoporosis means high fracture risk.

→ Install a low-profile beveled transition strip (under 1/4 inch) or level the subfloor so there is a flush, continuous surface across the doorway/hallway junction.

CRITICAL entry Clutter/obstacles in walking path

Low black metal frame/rack object sitting directly on the floor in the walking path near the window, at ankle height and dark/low-contrast against the floor.

A low, hard-to-see metal object in the direct path of travel is a prime target for her front-wheeled walker to strike or for her to trip over, especially given her fear of falling and impaired mobility.

→ Remove this object from the walking path entirely or relocate it flush against a wall well outside the walker's turning radius.

CRITICAL stairs Stairs - handrails present and secure

A stairway is visible directly off the hallway with no handrail visible on the open side and no contrast marking on the steps

With osteoporosis and a front-wheeled walker, any stair without a secure handrail on both sides is a near-certain fall/fracture risk given her fear of standing and limited standing tolerance.

→ Block patient access to this stairway entirely; ensure her actual living space routes avoid stairs, or install bilateral handrails and high-contrast step edge tape if unavoidable.

CRITICAL entry Floor surfaces - even, unobstructed walking path

Bare concrete floor with patchy, uneven discoloration, debris/dust, and what appears to be an unfinished or repaired section with a visible seam/ridge in the walking path

An uneven floor seam or debris patch can catch a front-wheeled walker's caster and cause a fall that, given her osteoporosis, is very likely to result in a fragility fracture.

→ Repair/level the floor surface, sweep and remove all loose debris, and confirm a smooth continuous path before she uses this route with her walker.

CRITICAL entry Cords/wires out of walking path

Electrical cord plugged into a wall outlet running along the baseboard near the walking path

A loose cord near the path is a classic snag hazard for a walker wheel or her foot, and any fall risks a hip or vertebral fracture given her osteoporosis.

→ Secure or reroute the cord along the wall with cord covers/tape well outside the walking path, or eliminate the need for the cord in this area.

CRITICAL entry floors - condition/evenness of walking surface

Entry/hallway floor is unfinished/patched concrete with visible seams, discolored patches, and uneven texture transitions running the length of the corridor

Uneven seams and patch edges in the flooring can catch a front-wheeled walker's caster or her foot, and with osteoporosis a resulting fall is likely to cause a fragility fracture.

→ Level and finish the flooring (smooth patching or replace with a continuous non-slip surface) before move-in; if unfinished during interim use, block off area until repaired.

CRITICAL entry clutter/obstacles in walking path

A-frame sign/display stand knocked over and lying across the walking path

A fallen object directly in the walker's path is a tripping/obstruction hazard that could cause an unrecoverable fall given her limited standing tolerance and fear of standing.

→ Remove the fallen stand immediately and keep the walkway completely clear.

CRITICAL entry Clear walking paths of obstacles

A low black metal frame/stand object sits directly in the walking path near the window wall, partially blocking the hallway width.

An object protruding into the path can catch a 28in front-wheeled walker or Monica's feet, and with osteoporosis a resulting fall carries high fracture risk.

→ Remove or relocate the metal stand out of the walking corridor entirely before discharge.

CRITICAL entry Flooring transitions/thresholds

Abrupt transition/seam between bare concrete flooring and adjacent carpet, with a visible raised edge/lip at the junction.

A raised transition strip is a classic catch point for front-wheeled walkers and a common fall trigger; for her, this likely means a hip or vertebral fracture.

→ Install a beveled, low-profile transition strip flush with both surfaces, or replace flooring for a seamless transition.

MODERATE entry Clutter/obstacles in walking path

Freestanding metal frame/stand (appears to be a sign or rack base) positioned in the walkway near the window

An object protruding into the path narrows clearance for her 28-inch walker and could be struck or tripped over given her fear of standing and limited balance.

→ Remove or relocate the stand out of the primary walking path, ensuring at least 36 inches of clear walker width.

MODERATE entry Doorway thresholds

Doorway frames appear to provide adequate width for passage, but no threshold ramp or leveling visible at the door transitions

Any raised or uneven threshold at a doorway is a common catch point for a front-wheeled walker, increasing fall/fracture risk for someone deconditioned and fearful of standing.

→ Inspect and, if needed, install a beveled threshold ramp at each doorway to eliminate any lip greater than 1/4 inch.

MODERATE entry Handrail/support availability in hallways

No grab bars, handrails, or wall-mounted support visible along this hallway/entry route

Without any support surface along the walking route, she has nothing to steady herself on if she becomes fatigued or unsteady mid-walk, which is likely given her limited standing tolerance.

→ Install a continuous handrail along long hallway stretches she must traverse at home, especially near any transition points.

MODERATE entry Floor condition/surface hazards

Large area of exposed, unfinished subfloor with visible adhesive residue, staining, and an uneven, patchy surface texture in the hallway leading to the unit.

An unfinished, sticky/uneven subfloor can catch a walker's wheels or create an inconsistent rolling surface, increasing fall risk for someone with limited standing tolerance and fear of standing.

→ Complete flooring installation (finish flooring or replace with continuous slip-resistant surface) before patient move-in; ensure no adhesive residue remains in the walking path.

MODERATE entry Clutter/obstacles in walking path

A black metal frame/rack (appears to be a luggage or storage stand) is positioned in the hallway near the window, encroaching into the walking path close to the doorway.

An object partially obstructing the corridor could narrow clearance below her 28-inch walker width or become a bump/snag hazard while she maneuvers near the doorway.

→ Remove or relocate the metal rack out of the hallway entirely so the full walker width has clear, unobstructed passage.

MODERATE entry Entryway/door accessibility

Heavy black self-closing door at the hallway entrance with a visible raised door threshold/frame at floor level.

A heavy door with a raised threshold requires reaching/pushing while balancing on a walker and stepping over a lip, both risky for a deconditioned patient with limited standing tolerance.

→ Adjust door closer tension for easier opening, and add a beveled threshold ramp if the door frame has any floor-level lip; consider automatic door opener if this is her unit's primary entrance.

MODERATE entry Floor surfaces/transitions between rooms

Abrupt transition from carpet to bare, stained hard flooring at threshold between rooms, with visible lip/edge where materials meet.

A raised or uneven transition strip can catch the front wheel of her walker or her feet, and with osteoporosis even a stumble here risks a fragility fracture.

→ Install a low-profile beveled transition strip flush with both surfaces, or remove the lip entirely; ensure walker wheels roll smoothly across the junction.

MODERATE entry Doorway/pathway width for mobility device

Narrow hallway/doorway passage with dark door frame and adjacent low metal rack, appearing to leave limited clearance for a 28-inch-wide walker.

If the passage is narrower than her walker's width plus hand clearance, she may need to angle or squeeze through, increasing fall risk during transfers through doorways.

→ Measure actual doorway/hallway clearance; if under ~32 inches, consider removing obstructing furniture/racks and confirm the path accommodates the walker with margin.

MODERATE entry Doorways - ease of opening/closing, clearance

Heavy, self-closing black hinged door in the direct walking path with no visible door-closer delay or lever-style easy-pull hardware

A heavy door that swings shut can strike or off-balance a deconditioned walker user who needs both hands on the walker, risking a fall while she is mid-transfer through the doorway.

→ Install a door closer with adjustable slow-close delay or a lightweight lever handle within easy reach; consider a door-stop/hold-open device for her main routes.

MODERATE entry Floors - even, non-slip walking surfaces

Hallway flooring is bare, rough-textured/unfinished concrete-like surface with visible surface variation and discoloration patches

An uneven or inconsistently textured floor surface can catch or destabilize the front wheels of her walker, increasing fall risk during ambulation.

→ Replace with a smooth, non-slip, level flooring surface (e.g., low-pile commercial vinyl) along her primary walking path; avoid unfinished/rough concrete in transit areas.

MODERATE entry Clutter/obstacles in pathway

Freestanding narrow-legged tripod signage stands and a small round high-top table placed within the hallway walking path

These narrow, low-visibility metal legs can catch a walker's front wheels or be missed with her limited mobility and fear of standing, increasing fall risk in a corridor she needs clear for safe ambulation.

→ Remove or relocate freestanding signage stands and tables to the side, out of the primary walking/transfer path, ensuring at least 36 inches of clear walker width.

MODERATE entry Floor surfaces - clean, debris-free

Additional debris and residue accumulation visible further down the same corridor floor

Loose debris on the floor can catch under walker wheels or cause a slip; for this deconditioned, osteoporotic patient this raises fracture risk with repeated use of this hallway.

→ Thoroughly clean and inspect the entire corridor floor for debris before patient uses this path independently.

MODERATE entry adequate clearance width in hallways

Tall tripod-legged tablet/sign stand positioned in the corridor, narrowing the clear walking width

Tripod legs projecting into the path can catch the 28-inch-wide walker's wheels or reduce clearance below what she needs to maneuver safely.

→ Relocate the stand flush against the wall or remove it from the through-path to preserve at least 36 inches of clear walker width.

MODERATE entry Floor surface condition/trip hazards

Entry/hallway floor is bare, unfinished concrete with heavy staining, discoloration, and visible surface irregularities/patching along the main walking path.

Uneven or rough patches in the concrete could catch a walker wheel or foot, risking a fall that would likely cause a fragility fracture given her osteoporosis.

→ Have flooring inspected and leveled/repaired or covered with a secured, low-pile non-slip surface before move-in.

MODERATE entry Adequate hallway lighting

Hallway is lit primarily by natural light from the side windows; no ceiling or wall light fixtures are visible in the corridor.

Without supplemental lighting, this hallway will be dim at night or on cloudy days, increasing risk of a misstep given her fear of standing and reliance on a walker.

→ Install overhead or plug-in nightlights along the hallway providing even illumination end-to-end.

LOW entry Floor surface consistency

Floor surface transitions from carpet to a hard, glossy tile-like surface near the doorway

A change in floor texture/friction can subtly alter walker wheel resistance and unsteady her gait, though the surface itself appears intact and non-slip in this frame.

→ Confirm the hard flooring has a matte, non-slip finish and that the transition strip is flush and secure.

LOW entry Floor surface condition in walking path

Long stretch of bare concrete/stone flooring with heavy staining, discoloration, and surface texture variation throughout the hallway.

The staining appears largely cosmetic rather than a true elevation change, but any hidden unevenness in worn flooring is still a concern for a deconditioned walker user.

→ Inspect flooring surface for actual unevenness or cracks (not just discoloration); repair any uneven patches before move-in.

LOW entry Support rails in transit areas

No handrail, grab bar, or wall-mounted support surface visible along the entry hallway walls.

While not a standard requirement for a hallway, the absence of any support surface means she has nothing to steady herself on if she becomes unsteady mid-transit between rooms.

→ Consider a wall-mounted handrail along the long hallway stretch given her fear of standing and deconditioning.

LOW entry Furniture/obstacles - clearance along walking path

Low decorative railing/edge structure visible at window ledge, not part of the walking path but near the corridor edge

This fixture is not in her direct walker path and does not appear to obstruct movement, but proximity to an open edge should be confirmed as guarded.

→ No action needed if this area is not part of her accessible route; verify barrier is secure if she will pass near it.

CLEAR entry Lighting adequacy in walking areas

Hallway is well-lit with natural light from large windows and overhead fixtures; illuminated exit sign visible

Good ambient lighting reduces her risk of misjudging floor transitions or obstacles while using her walker.

→ Maintain current lighting; ensure any night lighting in her actual home is equally adequate.

CLEAR entry Lighting adequacy

Hallway is evenly lit with visible overhead lighting and a clearly illuminated exit sign; no dim or shadowed areas observed along the corridor.

Adequate, even lighting reduces the chance of misjudging floor transitions or obstacles, which is protective given her fall risk.

→ No action needed; maintain current lighting levels.

CLEAR entry Lighting adequacy in hallway

Entry/hallway is well lit by large windows providing ample natural daylight across the walking path.

Good natural lighting reduces visual hazards during daytime transit for this patient with limited standing tolerance and fear of falling.

→ No action needed for daytime; confirm adequate artificial lighting is also present for evening/night use.

CLEAR entry Lighting - adequate illumination in hallways

Hallway is well lit by overhead fixtures and large arched windows providing ample natural daylight

Adequate, even lighting reduces the risk of misjudging floor transitions or obstacles, which is beneficial given her fear of falling.

→ Maintain current lighting levels; ensure equivalent lighting is present at night with accessible switches.

CLEAR entry Lighting adequacy

Corridor is well-lit by large windows/skylight providing ample natural daylight

Adequate lighting reduces her risk of misjudging floor irregularities or obstacles while using her walker.

→ No action needed; maintain similar lighting levels for evening/night use with supplemental fixtures.

CLEAR entry Rugs/mats secured or absent

No loose area rugs or mats visible in the corridor

Absence of throw rugs removes a common walker-catch and slip hazard for her.

→ Continue to avoid placing unsecured rugs/mats in her walking paths at home.

CLEAR entry lighting adequacy in hallways

Hallway is well-lit by large glass-block windows and overhead fixtures with no visible dim/shadowed areas

Adequate, even lighting reduces her risk of misjudging the uneven floor surface while using the walker.

→ Maintain current lighting levels; no action needed.

CLEAR entry throw rugs/mats in path

No area rugs, mats, or loose floor coverings present in the corridor

Absence of loose rugs removes a common walker-catch/trip hazard for this patient.

→ No action needed; avoid adding unsecured rugs in this transition space.

CLEAR entry Walker/wheelchair clearance in pathways

Hallway width appears adequate (unobstructed aside from the noted stand) for a 28in walker to pass through comfortably.

Sufficient clearance in this stretch supports safe walker use once the loose object is removed.

→ No action needed beyond removing the noted obstruction.

5 · Patient-reported context (voice walkthrough)

Caregiver: Okay, I'm ready.

Agent: Great, let's start right at the entry door. As you head in, I want to flag that carpet-to-concrete transition — that uneven edge could easily catch her walker wheel, so we may want a ramp or beveled strip there. Can you tell me, does Monica usually pause or lean on anything right when she first comes through the door?

Caregiver: Um, maybe this edge here.

Agent: Got it, so that edge she's leaning on isn't actually a stable support — just the wall or floor line. Since there's no grab bar there, it'd be worth adding something sturdy for her to grip right at that spot. Let's keep moving down the hallway toward the bathroom — how's the lighting and path looking as you walk?

Caregiver: We have really big windows here, so it, it works out well.

Agent: That's good, natural light helps a lot with fall risk. I do want to flag that overhead wiring and fixtures I'm seeing in this hallway ceiling — not a walker issue, but if she ever looks up or reaches, let's make sure it's tucked away safely. As we head toward the bathroom, where does Monica usually steady herself at night on this walk — any furniture or wall she uses?

Caregiver: Uh, maybe these walls here.

Agent: Okay, just the walls, no rail — that's worth noting since drywall won't hold her weight if she stumbles. I also see a power cord crossing the hallway floor here, that's a real catch-point for her walker wheel, so that needs to be taped down or rerouted before Friday. Let's keep going into the bathroom now, since that's the spot I most want to check carefully.
